section.title: The Marriage of the Lamb
section.sectionRationale: Chapter 18 ended with the great prostitute Babylon in ashes and the merchants of the earth weeping over her ruin. Chapter 19 opens the seam: heaven itself answers with a fourfold Hallelujah, the only four times that word appears in the whole New Testament, and the scene pivots from a judged prostitute to a prepared bride. The unit closes when John, overwhelmed, falls to worship the revealing angel and is firmly redirected to God alone, a clean hinge before the rider on the white horse appears in the next scene.
section.thesis: Heaven answers the fall of Babylon with an outburst of praise, then announces the wedding the whole story of Scripture has been moving toward: the Lamb takes His bride, clothed not in her own achievement but in the righteous acts He gives her to do, and even the angel who announces it insists that God alone is to be worshiped.
section.frameNote: Five beats: heaven's fourfold Hallelujah over Babylon's judgment; the voice from the throne calling every servant of God to praise; the announcement of the marriage of the Lamb and the bride made ready; the beatitude on those invited to the supper; and the angel's refusal of worship, pointing every eye back to God.

units.label: Heaven's Hallelujah
units.note: praise for the judgment of the prostitute

verses.text: After this I heard a sound like the roar of a great multitude in heaven, shouting: “{{allelouia|Hallelujah}}! Salvation and glory and power belong to our God!
For His judgments are true and just. He has judged the great prostitute who corrupted the earth with her immorality. He has avenged the blood of His servants that was poured out by her hand.”
And a second time they called out: “Hallelujah! Her smoke rises forever and ever.”
And the twenty-four elders and the four living creatures fell down and worshiped God who sits on the throne, saying: “Amen, Hallelujah!”

units.label: Praise our God, small and great

verses.text: Then a voice came from the throne, saying: “Praise our God, all you who serve Him, and those who fear Him, small and great alike!”
And I heard a sound like the roar of a great multitude, like the rushing of many waters, and like a mighty rumbling of thunder, crying out: “Hallelujah! For the Lord our God the Almighty reigns.
Let us rejoice and be glad and give Him the glory. For the {{gamos|marriage}} of the Lamb has come, and His bride has made herself ready.
She was given clothing of fine linen, bright and pure.” For the fine linen she wears is the {{dikaiomata|righteous acts}} of the saints.
Then the angel told me to write, “Blessed are those who are invited to the marriage supper of the Lamb.” And he said to me, “These are the true words of God.”

So I fell at his feet to {{proskyneo|worship}} him. But he told me, “Do not do that! I am a fellow servant with you and your brothers who rely on the {{martyria|testimony}} of Jesus. Worship God! For the testimony of Jesus is the spirit of prophecy.”

allelouia.gloss: Hallelujah, a Greek transliteration of the Hebrew hallelu Yah, praise the LORD. It occurs only here in the entire New Testament, four times in six verses, as heaven bursts into praise over the judgment of Babylon and the coming marriage of the Lamb.

gamos.gloss: Marriage or wedding. Scripture has long pictured God's covenant with His people as a marriage; here the picture reaches its resolution as the Lamb's wedding day arrives and His bride, the redeemed, is finally and fully joined to Him.

dikaiomata.gloss: Righteous acts or righteous deeds, the plural of dikaioma. Distinct from dikaiosyne, the righteousness credited to believers by faith, this word names the actual good deeds the saints do, deeds the text says the bride is given to wear, grace producing genuine fruit.

martyria.gloss: Testimony or witness. The testimony of Jesus, held by His people even to death, is here called the spirit of prophecy, the animating center of every true prophetic word, gathering the whole book of Revelation around the witness to Christ.

proskyneo.gloss: To worship, literally to fall down and kiss toward, an act of prostration and homage. John instinctively offers it to the dazzling angel; the angel's sharp refusal, twice repeated in Revelation, insists that this act belongs to God alone.

basic.context: Babylon, the great prostitute, has just fallen in chapter 18. Now heaven erupts in a fourfold shout of Hallelujah, praising God for judging her truly and justly. Then the scene turns joyful: the marriage of the Lamb has come, and His bride, the redeemed people of God, is dressed not in her own effort but in righteous acts she is given to wear. An angel pronounces those invited to the wedding supper blessed, and when John falls down to worship him, the angel refuses, insisting worship belongs to God alone.
basic.hermeneutics: This scene answers Babylon's fall with heaven's praise and turns immediately to a wedding, a deliberate contrast the whole chapter is built on. Readers debate exactly who the bride is, the church, the true Israel, or both together, and whether the bride's fine linen is purely a gift or also the visible fruit of a transformed life; Scripture holds both together, grace that produces real, good works. What is not in doubt is the center: judgment falls, joy rises, and every impulse to worship anyone but God is refused.
basic.originalLanguages: Four times the word allelouia rings out, hallelu Yah, the only place it appears in the whole New Testament. The gamos, the marriage, of the Lamb has come, and the bride wears the dikaiomata, the righteous acts, of the saints, given to her rather than woven by her own hands. When John tries to proskyneo, worship, the angel who bears the martyria, the testimony, of Jesus, he is refused and pointed back to God.
basic.typology: The marriage supper of the Lamb fulfills a long biblical pattern, God as bridegroom and His people as bride, running from the prophets' pictures of Israel's unfaithfulness to Paul's teaching that marriage itself is a picture of Christ and the church. The bride's fine linen, given rather than self-woven, points to a righteousness that is first received before it is lived out.
basic.christ: This is the Lamb's wedding day, the goal toward which the whole story of Scripture has been moving. The Bridegroom who was slain has judged the prostitute who corrupted the earth and now takes to Himself a bride made ready, not by her own effort, but by what He gives her to wear. His testimony is called the spirit of all true prophecy, the center that every word from God finally points to.
basic.god: God is praised as true and just in judgment, and as the one whose reign is the ground of all rejoicing, small and great alike are called to praise Him. When an overwhelmed John tries to worship the angel instead, the angel's sharp refusal protects what matters most in this whole scene, that worship belongs to God and to Him only.
basic.crossReferences: Isaiah, Hosea, and Ezekiel all picture God's covenant with His people as a marriage, often a strained one. Ephesians 5 teaches that human marriage itself pictures Christ and the church. And Revelation 22 later repeats the same refusal of worship offered to an angel, showing this is not a one time slip but a settled pattern in the book.
basic.oneStory: The story that began with a bride corrupted by unfaithfulness in the prophets now ends with a bride made ready, clothed in righteous acts she did not weave herself. Babylon's judgment and the Lamb's wedding are two sides of the same turning point, and the scene closes by insisting that every ounce of the glory, and the worship, belongs to God alone.

context.literary: This unit is the hinge between the fall of Babylon, narrated across chapters 17 and 18, and the appearing of the rider on the white horse in the rest of chapter 19. Heaven's fourfold Hallelujah answers the mourning of the merchants and kings over Babylon's smoke, and the marriage of the Lamb answers, point for point, the picture of the great prostitute: judgment on the one, joy over the other.
context.historical: Written to churches under real pressure to compromise with an empire that styled itself divine, the vision assures suffering believers that the seductive, corrupting power arrayed against them will be judged, and that their faithfulness, however costly, is headed toward a wedding feast, not a dead end.
context.canonical: The marriage supper of the Lamb gathers up the Bible's long marriage imagery for God and His people, and the beatitude on those invited echoes other beatitudes scattered through Revelation, each one meant to steady readers moving through visions of judgment toward the promised joy at the end.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To answer the fall of corrupting, seductive Babylon with heaven's praise, to announce the long awaited marriage of the Lamb and His prepared bride, and to guard, sharply and repeatedly, against worship being directed at anyone or anything other than God.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The passage describes a scene of worship and announcement in heaven; it does not prescribe a liturgy to copy. Its abiding, binding truths are that God's judgments are true and just, that His people are being prepared for full union with Christ, and that worship belongs to God alone, a command every reader is meant to obey.

debates.question: Who exactly is the bride, and who are the invited guests?

views.label: The bride is the whole redeemed church
views.case: Revelation elsewhere calls the church the bride of Christ, and Ephesians 5 identifies the church as Christ's bride; on this reading the marriage supper's invited guests are simply a way of describing the same people from another angle, guests and bride together.

views.label: The bride is distinguished from the invited guests
views.case: Some readers, especially in dispensational traditions, distinguish a bride, often identified with the church, from guests invited to the feast, sometimes associated with believing Israel or Old Testament saints, taking the invitation language in verse 9 at face value as naming a separate group.

debates.bearing: Either way, the text's own emphasis is pastoral rather than taxonomic: everyone united to the Lamb by faith belongs at this wedding, whether pictured as bride or guest, and all are called blessed.

debates.question: Are the righteous acts of the saints something the bride achieves, or something she is given?

views.label: Given, a gift of grace
views.case: Verse 8 says plainly she was given the fine linen to wear; the passive voice points to a righteousness that originates outside her, granted rather than self-produced, in keeping with the gospel's insistence that we are clothed by God, not by our own effort, echoing Genesis 3:21 and Isaiah 61:10.

views.label: Genuine deeds, really done
views.case: The same verse calls the linen the righteous acts, plural deeds, of the saints, real actions performed by real people; Scripture elsewhere insists that grace produces visible fruit, and this clothing pictures a transformed life, not a legal fiction.

debates.bearing: The two are not in tension: grace that justifies also sanctifies, so the bride's linen is at once a gift she receives and a life she is enabled to live, faith working through love.

study.originalLanguages: The chapter is framed by allelouia, hallelu Yah, praise the LORD, appearing nowhere else in the New Testament but four times here, as if the whole book has been saving up this word for Babylon's fall and the Lamb's wedding. The gamos, the marriage, arrives, and the bride is clothed in dikaiomata, righteous acts, given rather than woven by her own hand. When John's instinct is to proskyneo, prostrate himself before the dazzling angel who bears the martyria, the testimony, of Jesus, the angel's refusal is immediate and firm: that word and that posture belong to God alone.

typology.type: God and His people as bride and bridegroom
typology.fulfillment: The prophets' pictures of a wayward bride, Israel, are answered here in a bride finally, fully made ready for her Bridegroom, the Lamb.
As God clothed Adam and Eve after the fall, Genesis 3:21, so here the bride is clothed by grace, her righteous acts a gift before they are a labor.

typology.type: The marriage supper as the goal of the story
typology.fulfillment: Every biblical wedding feast and banquet of celebration points forward to this one, the joy set before the whole people of God at the end of the age.

study.christ: This is the Lamb's own wedding day, the point toward which redemptive history has always been moving. The Bridegroom who was slain, and whose blood judged the prostitute who corrupted the earth, now takes to Himself a bride made ready, clothed in righteous acts that are given to her, not earned by her. His testimony, held onto by martyrs and saints alike, is called the very spirit of prophecy, meaning every true word from God, in every age, ultimately points to Him.
study.god: God is praised as true and just, His judgments finally vindicated before all creation, and His reign is the reason every voice, small and great, is called to praise. When John, overwhelmed by the vision, falls to worship the revealing angel, God's messenger sharply refuses the honor and insists it belongs to God alone, a small scene that guards the whole book's center of gravity: worship God.

crossReferences.note: God clothes His people with garments of salvation, a robe of righteousness, like a bride adorned, the same picture of given, not self-made, clothing.
Christ loves the church and gives Himself for her to present her to Himself in splendor, without spot or wrinkle, the marriage imagery Revelation 19 brings to completion.
Jesus' parable of the wedding banquet and its invited guests anticipates this scene's beatitude on those invited to the marriage supper of the Lamb.
John again falls to worship an angel and is again refused, the same guardrail repeated to make sure the point is not missed.

study.oneStory: The story that began with a bride corrupted by unfaithfulness now ends with a bride made ready, not by her own weaving, but by what the Lamb gives her to wear. Babylon's judgment and the wedding of the Lamb are two halves of the same turning point in the story of the world, and even in this moment of overwhelming joy, the scene insists on one thing above all: the glory, and the worship, belong to God alone.

sermon.title: The Wedding Feast of the Lamb
sermon.why: Preaching directly on Revelation 19:1-10, Keller traces the single story of the Bible to its resolution in this vision, working through the prostitute and the problem, the Lamb and the solution, and the bride and the result, showing how every plotline of history is tied up in Jesus.

quiz.q: What does heaven's chorus in verses 1 to 3 celebrate?

options.text: God's true and just judgment on the great prostitute who corrupted the earth and shed His servants' blood.
options.note: Verse 2 states this explicitly as the reason for the praise.

options.text: The fall of a wealthy, seductive city, without reference to why it fell.
options.note: The city's fall is in view, but the text roots the praise in God's just judgment, not the ruin by itself.

options.text: The arrival of the thousand year reign described later in the book.
options.note: That scene comes in chapter 20; this chorus responds to Babylon's judgment.

options.text: The coronation of the beast as king over the earth.
options.note: The beast is judged elsewhere in Revelation, never celebrated; this chorus praises God.

quiz.q: What is the bride given to wear, and what does it represent, according to verses 7 and 8?

options.text: Fine linen, bright and pure, which the text identifies as the righteous acts of the saints.
options.note: Verse 8 states this directly.

options.text: Fine linen she wove entirely by her own effort, apart from any gift.
options.note: The text says she was given the clothing, pointing to grace as the source, even though the linen pictures real deeds.

options.text: Royal purple, symbolizing worldly wealth and status.
options.note: The color named is not purple but fine linen, bright and pure.

options.text: Sackcloth, symbolizing mourning and repentance.
options.note: The scene is one of joy and readiness, not mourning.

quiz.q: What happens when John falls at the angel's feet to worship him in verse 10?

options.text: The angel refuses worship, calls himself a fellow servant, and tells John to worship God instead.
options.note: This is exactly what the angel says in verse 10.

options.text: The angel accepts the worship gladly as heaven's chief messenger.
options.note: The opposite happens; the angel firmly refuses.

options.text: The angel says nothing and simply continues speaking.
options.note: The angel responds directly and pointedly to John's action.

options.text: The angel rebukes John for being present in the vision at all.
options.note: The rebuke concerns the act of worship, not John's presence.

quiz.q: According to verse 10, what is called 'the spirit of prophecy'?

options.text: The testimony of Jesus.
options.note: The verse states this directly: the testimony of Jesus is the spirit of prophecy.

options.text: The words of the twenty-four elders.
options.note: The elders appear earlier in the chapter, but this phrase is tied to the testimony of Jesus.

options.text: The book of the law given through Moses.
options.note: The verse does not mention the law here; it names the testimony of Jesus.

options.text: The angel's own personal message.
options.note: The angel points away from himself to the testimony of Jesus.

quiz.q: Who is pronounced blessed in verse 9?

options.text: Those who are invited to the marriage supper of the Lamb.
options.note: This is the exact wording of the beatitude in verse 9.

options.text: Those who host and prepare the marriage supper.
options.note: The blessing falls on the invited guests, not on those preparing the feast.

options.text: The twenty-four elders alone.
options.note: The elders are mentioned earlier in the chapter, but the beatitude names those invited, more broadly.

options.text: The servants of the great prostitute.
options.note: Her servants are not in view here; the blessing is on those invited to the Lamb's supper.

questions: Heaven bursts into Hallelujah over God's true and just judgment. Is there an injustice in your own life or in the world that you find hard to trust God with? What would it look like to let this scene shape your prayers about it?
The bride is given her fine linen; she does not weave it herself. Where are you tempted to think your standing with God depends on your own effort rather than on what He has given you in Christ?

The same linen is called the righteous acts of the saints, real deeds done by real people. How does knowing your good works flow from grace, rather than earning it, change how you go about doing them this week?
John falls to worship the angel almost by reflex, and is firmly redirected. What things, good things even, do you find your heart drifting toward worshiping instead of God?

Those invited to the marriage supper of the Lamb are called blessed. How does picturing your future as an invitation to a wedding feast, rather than merely an escape from judgment, change the way you long for Christ's return?

translationNotes.note: Hallelujah transliterates the Hebrew hallelu Yah, praise the LORD. This is its only appearance anywhere in the New Testament, and it occurs four times within these six verses, a concentrated burst found nowhere else in Scripture's Greek text.
The great prostitute refers back to Babylon, described at length in chapters 17 and 18; the judgment celebrated here is the direct sequel to her fall.

Has made herself ready uses a verb that can carry the sense of readiness achieved, yet verse 8 immediately clarifies that even her clothing was given to her, holding together human readiness and divine gift.

The righteous acts of the saints translates dikaiomata, a different Greek word from dikaiosyne, the righteousness credited to believers by faith; this word names actual deeds, the visible fruit that grace produces.

meta.notes: BSB text supplied and pre-verified this session for Revelation 19:1-10. No em or en dashes were present in the supplied text and none were introduced. Straight quotation marks in the supplied text were rendered as curly quotation marks to match this repository's typographic convention; no wording was altered.
